text: We present a video of a synchronous laparoscopic left lateral sectionectomy with a laparoscopic right hemicolectomy for a cecal carcinoma presenting with a synchronous liver metastasis. The case is a seventy-five year old gentleman who presented to our unit with a cecal adenocarcinoma which was detected through the UK's bowel cancer screening programme. He was found to have a synchronous left lobe metastasis. A CT scan of his abdomen shows a cecal tumour indicated here by the red arrow, and the liver metastasis in segment three is best demonstrated on the MR scan, again as indicated here by the red arrow.

The surgical team places two 12mm and two 5mm ports for the laparoscopic procedure. The left lateral sectionectomy begins with laparoscopy to identify the liver metastasis. The left liver is mobilized by dividing the left triangular ligament using a hook diaphragm, with a swab placed under the liver for protection. Nylon tape is used for traction during liver parenchyma transection. The left coronary ligament is divided to the left hepatic vein. Intraoperative ultrasound confirms the metastatic disease position. The transection line is marked at the falciform ligament level. The liver parenchyma is transected using the Lotus liver resector, starting at the ligamentum teres. The left portal inflow is divided with a laparoscopic endo-GIA stapler. The transection continues into segment two, where the left hepatic vein is divided with the stapler. Haemostasis is checked, and haemostatic adjuncts are used as needed.

The surgeon performs a right hemicolectomy, beginning with the division of inflamed adhesions between the cecum, omentum, and anterior abdominal wall using a ligature energy device with a Maryland tip. The cecum is separated from the anterior abdominal wall, and the right colon is mobilized using a medial to lateral approach. The peritoneum is divided on the medial side of the mesocolon, achieving total mesocolic excision. Dissection proceeds to the origins of the ileocolic, middle colic, and right colic arteries, preserving the ureter and gonadal vessels. An omental resection is performed due to firm adhesions. The omentum is divided up to the transverse colon, which is moved cordially, exposing the lesser sac. Lateral dissection of the right colon is completed by dividing lateral peritoneal folds. The right colon is fully mobilized for resection and anastomosis. The duodenum, head of the pancreas, and secured ileocolic pedicle are identified. An endocatch device is inserted through a port to retrieve the liver specimen.
The surgeon secures the mobilized cecum with a Johan instrument. A small incision is made as a midline extension of the existing twelve millimeter port.
